events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The scenario describes a residential block/brick laying job at rough-complete stage. The observed condition is "bond pattern broken." Reference is TMS 402/602.

A broken bond pattern in masonry means the overlapping of units is not maintained properly. In standard running bond, units should overlap by at least 1/4 of the unit length (typically 2 inches for 8-inch block). A broken bond pattern is a non-compliant condition.

TMS 402/602 requires proper bonding - running bond should have at least 1/4 unit lap. If the bond pattern is broken, this is a defect that affects structural integrity and aesthetics.

Decision: fail (non-compliant)
Risk: medium - broken bond can affect structural integrity
Work stage: rough-complete
Component conditions: installed-defective, non-compliant
Percent complete: ~85% (rough-complete)
Value: I'll estimate the lap as less than required - maybe 0 (no lap) or some reduced lap

Let me construct the JSON.

    "Bond pattern broken — adjacent units do not maintain required lap/overlap, indicating a discontinuity in the running bond.",
    "Insufficient or no vertical head-joint offset between courses, compromising load distribution and wall integrity."
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Identify and mark all courses where bond is broken.",
    "Disassemble and relay affected units to restore proper running bond with minimum 1/4 unit length lap (typically 2 in. for 8 in. block).",
    "Re-inspect bond pattern after correction before proceeding to closure/finishing."
  ],
  "rationale": "TMS 402/602 requires masonry to be laid in running bond or other approved pattern with adequate lap. A broken bond pattern means the interlocking overlap between courses is lost, reducing the wall's ability to distribute loads and resist cracking. At rough-complete stage this is correctable but must be remediated before closure.",
  "references": ["TMS 402/602 — Masonry Structures: bonding/lap requirements for running bond", "TMS 602 — Specification for Masonry Structures: installation tolerances and bond pattern requirements"]
}
